name: Palio di Siena
tagline: Ninety seconds of bareback medieval fury as ten rival neighborhoods stake a year of honor on three laps of the Campo.
dates: Aug 16, 2026 (Palio dell'Assunta); Jul 2, 2027 (Palio di Provenzano) and Aug 16, 2027 — fixed annual dates, festival days run Jun 29–Jul 2 and Aug 13–16
history: Siena's central piazza has hosted public contests since the Middle Ages — brawling pugna matches, jousts, and by the 16th century bullfights staged by the city's neighborhood districts, the contrade. When the Grand Duke of Tuscany outlawed bullfighting in 1590, the contrade turned to racing: first on buffalo (bufalate), then donkeys, and finally horses. The first modern Palio run in a circuit around Piazza del Campo — the palio alla tonda — took place in 1633, and in 1729 the governor fixed the contrade at seventeen and set the rule that still holds today: only ten race each time, the seven left out of the previous year's edition plus three drawn by lot.

Two Palios are run each year, and both are religious feasts before they are races. The July 2 Palio di Provenzano honors the Madonna of Provenzano; the August 16 Palio dell'Assunta, added in 1701, celebrates the Assumption of Mary. Each race day caps four days of ritual — the tratta, in which horses are assigned to contrade by lottery, six trial runs (prove), candlelit contrada dinners, the blessing of horse and jockey inside the contrada church, and finally the Corteo Storico, a two-hour pageant of flag-throwers and drummers in Renaissance costume that circles the square before the start.

The race itself is gloriously ungoverned: ten jockeys ride bareback for three laps of the shell-shaped piazza — roughly 90 seconds — on a track of packed tufa clay laid over the paving stones, with mattress padding at the murderous San Martino corner. Almost anything is legal, jockeys whip each other as readily as their mounts, and a riderless horse (cavallo scosso) can win for its contrada. The lore runs deep: the contrada longest without a win is mocked as the nonna (grandmother); legendary jockey Andrea 'Aceto' Degortes took 14 Palios between 1964 and 1996; and only three contrade have ever pulled off the cappotto, sweeping both races in one year — most recently Lupa in 2016. This is no tourist re-enactment: for the Sienese, the Palio is the emotional center of civic life.
whyGo: It is the most intense minute and a half in European sport — a medieval city-state rivalry that never ended, played out at full gallop in one of the world's most beautiful squares. Standing in the Campo among 40,000 people as the mossa rope drops is free, unticketed, and unforgettable. Nothing else in Italy delivers this ratio of raw authenticity to zero admission cost.

gettingThere.route: Zürich HB → Milano Centrale (EC direct via Gotthard) → Firenze S.M.N. (Frecciarossa) → Siena (regional via Empoli, or faster Autolinee Toscane 131R bus from Florence)
gettingThere.notes: Book the Zürich–Milano EC as an SBB supersaver and the Milano–Firenze Frecciarossa on Trenitalia 2–4 months out; Siena's station is below the old town — take the escalators or a city bus up

gettingThere.route: Zürich (ZRH) → Florence (FLR) nonstop, then T2 tram to Firenze S.M.N. and train or 131R bus to Siena; Pisa (PSA) is the fallback
gettingThere.notes: Palio dates are peak season — lock in flights by early spring; last buses/trains back to Florence leave around 21:00–22:00, so sleep in or near Siena on race night

gettingThere.route: Zürich → Gotthard tunnel → Milano → A1 to Firenze → Firenze–Siena raccordo (about 600 km)
gettingThere.notes: Siena's center is a strict ZTL camera zone — park at Stadio/Il Campino or Santa Caterina lots outside the walls; expect road closures and full lots on race days, arrive by morning

costs.notes: Standing in the Campo is free, so the trip cost is driven almost entirely by lodging and seats: Siena hotels double or triple over Palio nights and sell out nearly a year ahead (thrifty = supersaver trains + a room in Poggibonsi or a Florence day-trip base), while balcony and grandstand (palco) seats overlooking the track run €350–700+ per person through agencies and private owners

booking.label: Official site — Comune di Siena Palio portal
Il Palio.org — encyclopedic reference (contrade, dates, results)

weather: Both dates land in the Tuscan high summer: expect 28–34°C, hammering sun and almost no rain, with August often the stickier of the two. The Campo's standing area has zero shade for the 4–6 hours you'll be penned in — bring a hat, sunscreen and more water than you think you need, and wear closed shoes for the packed crowd.
tips: Enter the center of the Campo early and for free — locals stake spots by early afternoon and access closes roughly two hours before the race; once you're in, there's no exit or re-entry and no toilets, so eat, drink and go beforehand.

Can't face the crush? Go to a prova instead — the six trial races on the preceding days have the same track, horses and contrada singing at a fraction of the crowd, and the evening prova generale is the most atmospheric.
Balcony, window and palco seats are sold by private owners and agencies, not the city — they start around €350 and sell out up to a year ahead, so book the moment you commit to the trip.
Book Siena lodging 9–12 months out or base yourself in Poggibonsi, Colle di Val d'Elsa or Florence; on race night the last transport out leaves before the post-race celebrations get going.

Eat with a contrada: the open-air cena della prova generale on the eve of the race seats thousands at long tables in the streets — tickets (€40–60) are sold through the contrada's own society and are the single best cultural buy in Siena.

Mind the etiquette: don't wear a contrada scarf you have no ties to, never cheer ironically or boo, and don't expect the race to run on time — the mossa (start) can take an hour of false starts, and that tension is part of the show.

localRecs.name: Osteria Le Logge
localRecs.note: Siena's classic address just off the Campo on Via del Porrione — pici, faraona and a deep Tuscan cellar; book well ahead on Palio days

localRecs.name: Antica Osteria Da Divo
localRecs.note: Tables set inside Etruscan-era cellars carved from the tufa near the Duomo — atmospheric splurge without tourist-menu laziness

localRecs.name: Torre del Mangia
localRecs.note: The 88 m tower over the Campo; climb it a non-race day for the definitive view of the shell-shaped piazza and the track line

localRecs.name: Duomo di Siena and Piccolomini Library
localRecs.note: One of Italy's great Gothic interiors — in late summer the extraordinary inlaid marble floor is often fully uncovered

localRecs.name: Nannini (Conca d'Oro)
localRecs.note: The historic Banchi di Sopra café for espresso, panforte and ricciarelli — the Sienese sweets to carry home

photos.caption: Jockeys ride bareback through a corner of Piazza del Campo during the July Palio
Horses at full gallop on the packed tufa-clay track laid over the Campo's paving, Palio dell'Assunta
The crowd packed into the center of Piazza del Campo on race day
